ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated 'cheese roll' entry; A0CKP (filled pastry) is the closest defensible parent analogue for a cheese-filled dough product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for cheese roll as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical cheese roll serving is approximately 60 g, equivalent to one individual roll as commonly sold at Singaporean bakeries and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Roll — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese Roll (record G00010352). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cheese pastry analogue (USDA SR Legacy #01009 cheddar cheese + pastry composite) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cheese Roll in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous food: cheese-filled pastry / savoury pastry with refined wheat dough, which falls in the low GI range (<=55) per Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed GI tables. Glycemic load per 60g serving = (55 x 8.85 x 0.60) / 100 = 2.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg per 100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cheese rolls; a single 60 g serving delivers approximately 767 mg sodium, exceeding the recommended sodium ceiling for a single meal, and regular consumption will substantially elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (8.85 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre and high fat content; refined pastry dough raises postprandial glucose despite low absolute carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume cheese rolls in small portions (60 g or less) occasionally, but should note that the complete absence of dietary fibre in the refined pastry dough accelerates glucose absorption, and the high saturated fat content (~13.8 g/100 g) impairs insulin sensitivity with regular intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 13.8 g/100 g, sodium 1279 mg/100 g, and cholesterol 78 mg/100 g collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cheese rolls are not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to the combination of high saturated fat, very high sodium, and moderate dietary cholesterol, all of which are established risk factors for atherosclerosis and hypertensive cardiovascular events.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~13.8 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; sodium 1279 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold, promoting systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cheese rolls are unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the high saturated fat activates pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) and the very high sodium worsens tissue oedema and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Roll is a savoury baked or fried pastry snack popular in Singapore, consisting of a cheese filling — typically processed or cheddar-style cheese — encased in a refined wheat-flour dough that is rolled, shaped, and either baked until golden or deep-fried. The texture is crisp and flaky on the outside with a dense, salty, and creamy cheese interior. It is commonly sold at Singaporean bakeries, food courts, and school canteens as a snack or light meal item. Nutritionally, cheese rolls are energy-dense at 307 kcal per 100 g, with high fat (23.06 g), high protein (16.09 g), and moderate carbohydrate (8.85 g). The sodium content is exceptionally high at 1279 mg per 100 g, making this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 13.8 g per 100 g from the cheese and butter components, exceeding recommended limits for heart health and rheumatoid arthritis management. Calcium content is notably high at 1375 mg per 100 g, reflecting the cheese component, which may benefit bone health. Dietary fibre is absent (0 g), limiting its value for glycaemic management. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on the refined wheat pastry component. Cheese rolls are not suitable for hypertension or cardiovascular disease management and should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cheese roll?
Is cheese roll high in sodium?
What is a cheese roll made of?
Is cheese roll suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in a cheese roll?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010352) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA SR Legacy cheddar cheese and pastry composite as HPB does not report these sub-fractions; this reduces sub-fraction confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence for core macros. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database peer-reviewed tables.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; the refined pastry dough and processed cheese filling contribute no measurable dietary fibre. This is a confirmed zero value, not a data gap. Saturated fat (13.8 g), monounsaturated fat (6.2 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.1 g), and trans fat (0.3 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cheddar cheese (#01009) and butter-based pastry composite as HPB does not report fatty acid sub-fractions; these values are approximate. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this specific composite): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Singaporean cheese roll composite are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. The input food_group 'Meat and alternatives' and food_subgroup 'Beef' appear to be data entry errors in the source record; cheese roll is a dairy-based pastry snack and has been reclassified accordingly. The input cultural.primary_cuisine 'Middle Eastern' has been retained as a secondary influence note but the primary cuisine is Singaporean given the HPB source and SG region tag. The alias '24. Cheese Roll' from the input has been cleaned to 'Cheese Roll' as the numeral prefix appears to be a menu numbering artefact.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous' to 'analogous_estimate' to conform to allowed values.
Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with glycemic_index 55.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' — validator confirmed allowed values are only ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; 'analogous_estimate' is not a valid foodex2_match_type value.
